questions.question: What is the fundamental difference between AI agents and traditional software?
options: AI agents can adapt their behavior based on new information
AI agents are written in Python
questions.explanation: AI agents can perceive, decide, and adapt based on new information, unlike traditional programs that follow fixed instructions.

questions.question: Which type of agent maintains internal state to handle partially observable environments?
questions.explanation: Model-based agents maintain an internal state/model of the world to handle environments where not everything is directly observable.

questions.question: What is the correct order of the agent loop?
questions.explanation: The agent loop follows: Perceive (gather information), Decide (reason about action), Act (execute tools).

questions.question: How many hours per week can a Digital FTE work?
questions.explanation: A Digital FTE works 24/7, which equals 168 hours per week (24 x 7).

questions.question: What is the approximate cost reduction of a Digital FTE compared to human tutoring?
questions.explanation: Digital FTEs can reduce costs by approximately 99% compared to human tutoring ($0.25 vs $50+ per session).

questions.question: Which component is NOT part of a good prompt?
questions.explanation: Examples should be relevant and purposeful, not random. A good prompt includes role, context, instructions, format, and relevant examples.

questions.question: What is the role of a system prompt?
questions.explanation: System prompts act like a constitution - they define the overall behavior, role, tone, and boundaries for the AI.

questions.question: How many examples are typically recommended for few-shot prompting?
questions.explanation: Few-shot prompting typically uses 2-5 examples to guide the model's behavior effectively.

questions.question: What does chain of thought prompting improve?
questions.explanation: Chain of thought prompting dramatically improves accuracy on complex reasoning tasks by encouraging step-by-step thinking.

title: Agent Tools and Function Calling - Quiz

questions.question: What are agent tools?
options: External capabilities agents use to interact with the world
questions.explanation: Tools are external capabilities (web search, code execution, API calls) that extend an agent's abilities beyond text generation.

questions.question: What does function calling enable?
questions.explanation: Function calling is the mechanism by which agents invoke tools - sending structured requests and receiving results.

questions.question: What makes a good agent tool?
options: Is atomic and does one thing well
questions.explanation: Good tools are atomic (do one thing well), well-documented, and safe with proper input validation and error handling.

questions.question: What does RAG stand for?
questions.explanation: RAG stands for Retrieval Augmented Generation - combining information retrieval with text generation.

questions.question: What is the main benefit of RAG?
options: Reduced hallucinations and use of proprietary data
questions.explanation: RAG reduces hallucinations and enables agents to work with proprietary or domain-specific data.

questions.question: What is the correct RAG pipeline order?
questions.explanation: The RAG pipeline: Ingest documents, Embed into vectors, Index in database, Retrieve relevant chunks, Generate responses.

questions.question: Which type of memory tracks the current interaction?
questions.explanation: Short-term memory (conversation history) tracks the current interaction.

questions.question: Why is conversation history management important?
questions.explanation: LLMs have context window limits, so managing what history to keep is crucial for maintaining relevant context.

questions.question: What enables semantic recall in long-term memory?
questions.explanation: Vector databases store embeddings that enable semantic search and recall of relevant past information.

questions.question: What is a limitation of single agents that multi-agent systems overcome?
options: Single agents can only do one thing at a time
questions.explanation: Single agents can only do one thing at a time and have limited context windows. Multi-agent systems enable parallel execution and specialization.

questions.question: In which communication pattern does one agent transfer control to another?
questions.explanation: Handoff is when one agent transfers control to another agent.

questions.question: What does A2A protocol standardize?
questions.explanation: A2A (Agent-to-Agent) protocol standardizes how agents communicate and collaborate.

questions.question: What does MCP stand for?
questions.explanation: MCP stands for Model Context Protocol - an open standard for connecting AI models to external data and tools.

questions.question: What architecture does MCP follow?
questions.explanation: MCP follows a client-server architecture where the MCP client runs in the agent and MCP servers expose capabilities.

questions.question: What can MCP servers expose?
questions.explanation: MCP servers can expose resources (data sources), tools (actions), and prompts (templates).
